item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This skip mentions Prometheus Labs as its creator. I don't see why the hub shouldn't be linked.

Editing this:

Prometheus Labs Defensive Products

To this:

Prometheus Labs Defensive Products

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't this be somewhere else…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A TF2 or Half-Life 2 mod using Gary's Mod to make maps would definitely work, yeah. Idunno why I hadn't thought of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A Garry's Mod server and/or map would be a good place to start, at least for underground multiplayer. A lot of the environment would probably look Combine-ish anyway, and all of Half-Life 2's textures and models and whatnot are in Garry's Mod by default.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Listen. If anyone interested in making an SCP shooter game or a survival-horror game sees this, you need to use this skip. There are two very, very obvious, easy plots you can do with this and countless less-obvious routes you can take; the Mark I Infantry/-1 instances should be relatively simple-to-program AIs; you have clearly labelled boss characters: the -2 and -3 instances. You have two very easy settings that can be used as maps (the Sahara desert above and the Super Sekrit Compound below) that can be created with procedural generation. You have characters that can be used in multiplayer games (who wouldn't want to play as one of those tank things, or cooperate to take wave after wave of any of these things down?).

What would the plot look like? Two routes, both simple: the player character has been captured/trapped by the sapient residents of the facility (PC is either Foundation or Prometheus Labs personnel) and needs to escape, or the inhabitants of the facility have decided to expand their borders and increase their genetic diversity, capturing the Foundation site above and forcing the PC to go in guns blazing. You can even have those as the same game, with the first half being "Escape from spoopy cyberpunk!" and the second half being "Explode spoopy cyberpunk!" Or make it a two-player game with players alternating between characters and trying to assist each other in their attempt to escape/get rescued/blow shit up. These proposed plots don't even hit on whatever actual story you might want to tell, and a creative writer can come up with other stories.

If this is well-written and well-programmed, I can see it taking off and maybe even surpassing Containment Breach. I can also see it being made lazily and looking like dog shit, but that's a given with pretty much every video game premise. Even if it's just a wave-defense game made from a Halo 3 mod or something, I can work with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The footnote and the sentence after it are redundantly repetitive. Personally, I'd edit the bit about survivability out of the footnote and leave the in-paragraph instance; but that's just a matter of personal preference.

I'm on the fence with the Fallout-ness here. But at least it seems to have been done intentionally/knowingly. Or so I presume from the company slogan.

The ORBCOM "…will turn your Prometheus Arsenal into a complete, self-sustaining military branch." Truth in advertising! 40 years and still a force to be respected. They just don't make cyborg deathbots like they used to.

I understand why the Foundation isn't interested in a close quarters fight with an unknown number of these things; but it seems borderline negligent that they haven't even tried to fully investigate the interior. For all they know there's a big red "Clone-borg Production Emergency Stop" button and all they have to do to make the facility safe-class is push it.

Still and all, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article basically implies that there was a massive cold war/war building up between the United States and China. A war which the company was going to profit off of, and had built up a _ton_ of inventory for. And then the whole war thing got called off, leaving them with a crapton of inventory that no one was going to buy. (At least, this was what I got from it)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't quite understand the concept at work here, but by no means do I dislike the article. +1'd, though I feel like I'm missing something.

It's…a build-your-own-army company? Which is cool, don't get me wrong, but I don't see what it has to do with Nixon's visit to Peking? What happened to Youssef?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't worry. I don't give a second thought to W40K either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I increasingly feel like the only person in the universe without encyclopedic knowledge of Warhammer 40k (I had to look that up!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm torn. On the one hand, this is quite well executed. On the other hand: servitors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oops. I missed the "abandoned in '72" part, I was thinking 2013 dollars. >.< Late 60s/early 70s makes it more "Wow, good deal" instead of "That wouldn't even cover materials".

On rereading, +1. I'm a sucker for a solid hardware piece and this is that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still, you've got recruitment of soldiers at a grand or two per, and inflation from 1970s to now is only about 5X. I like the price list, but I'd suggest doing a bit of refinement, as recruitment + training costs significantly more than that. - I think recruitment costs are around 10-25K, depending on branch, without taking into account non-signing incentives. Might be worth bumping that a little?

Modern tanks have a production cost of around 3-8 million, depending on who is making them. (t-90s cost around 3-4 million, m1s around twice that.) Tanks produced in the relevant time period had an inflation adjusted cost of around 1/3 of modern tanks, so those prices are closer.

I really liked this, though it reminded me of the underground city/"utopia"/hive people scp. Don't remember which one that was, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the prices

Don't forget to adjust for inflation. These things were being produced around 1970, and were meant to be competatively priced.

Thanks for the feedback. It seems I may have to do something about that ad copy. Maybe make it a supplement or replace it with an exploration log… Mulling over my options. Dunno what I'll do yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only concrete beef with this article is that the prices are INSANELY cheap, like 10-20 times cheaper than their mundane counterparts. If that's intentional, disregard of course, but I got nothing from the article that would imply a reason for so drastically undercutting the competition, not to mention how they'd be able to afford to do so.

Otherwise it's a little less fantastic and a little more explainy than I tend to like and the series of communications at the end was kind of linear and matter-of-fact. No complaints about the technical execution, though.

No vote ATM, sitting on an upvote while I let it settle in my head some.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: maybe, but 2000 is better off without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it as a stand-alone article, but I think I liked it better when it was part of your SCP-2000 proposal. Just my $.02. Still, an awesome article, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice to see Prometheus being used. While I do think the note explains everything too readily, overall I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I really liked reading this one, at least up until the Prometheus Labs order form. Personally, I feel like it just explains too much and I would have preferred the SCP much more if its origins were vague. That said, it did like the notes between the Labs employees.

Overall, though, the way this builds tension as it describes each entity being more weird and more dangerous is excellent. For that, I think it still deserves a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You better PM me, author. +1'd though. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this and thought 'So it's a video game surivaval mode generator'. And you managed to play that straight and eerie. Bravo. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Prometheus is all about bringin' fire to the people… sometimes it's just from the end of a cannon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't recall Aperture creating armies of cyborg deathbots. Aperture Science was more replace your blood with gasoline or inject you with praying mantis DNA, for no other reason than to just to see what happens.

If you've cut yourself at all in the course of these tests, you may have noticed that your blood is pure gasoline - that's normal. We've been shooting you with an invisible beam that's supposed to turn blood into gasoline, so all that means is it's working.

"Those of you who volunteered to be injected with praying mantis DNA, I've got some good news and some bad news. Bad news is we're postponing those tests indefinitely. Good news is we've got a much better test for you: fighting an army of mantis men. Pick up a rifle and follow the yellow line. You'll know when the test starts."
―Cave Johnson

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This actually fits very well into my headcanon of Prometheus as evolving from more charitable devices into warlike things before they were destroyed. I think it complements the way the labs have evolved over time, both from an out-of-universe perspective and from my personal in-universe one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There isn't anything mechanically wrong with this article, but this unfortunately falls dead-on in the center of the Prometheus-as-Aperture-Science tradition that I've grown to absolutely hate. Two or three years ago, I probably would have liked it. Now, it just grates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I mentioned in chat, I really feel like this is a behind the scenes look at a unit producing building in an RTS game, which makes me happy. Can you imagine if you could have production problems like this in Starcraft?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed counting the letters in the black boxes and putting together the cohesive history you'd written here. Most satisfying!

Cannot fault a single shred of the feel of this. I did have to resist the temptation to gloss over a little on some of the descriptions of the soldiers/machines, though. Personally, I gained little from SCP-1637-2, and felt that the SCP-1637-3s could be consolidated into one, using the best bits (foetus in the machinery…goosebumps! Torso as a chassis element…creepy!), and removing the bits I felt pulled me out of the immersion a little (the unlimited ammo, the levitation…seemed too paranormal for an SCP that I felt gained a lot of its impact by being almost too believable).

Still, however…I could not +1 this enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

It's got a DOOM feel, a Half-Life/Black Mesa feel, and a Fallout feel. None of these are bad things.

PS Do not, under any circumastances, google "cyclocephaly."

EDIT: One extremely minor nitpick is that in:

…recovered from the administrative section of Sub-Level 1 seem to suggest the facility was constructed in 1965,

the "seem to" should be removed, it's far too passive for a clinical, factual document. "Suggest" is enough to show that the date is questionable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was already getting a heavy Fallout feeling from this, and then you had to go and lampshade it with the PL slogan. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Image for this SCP was posted on Wikimedia Commons by user "Piotrus" and has been licensed under CC/BY/SA/3.0. It can be found here.
